structured_choices.data_source: 50,000 high-resolution retinal fundus images collected from multiple outpatient clinics over a two-year period. Images sourced from a diverse set of clinical sites to ensure generalizability across settings.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary labels (presence/absence of diabetic retinopathy) derived from consensus expert ophthalmologist annotations. For images with disagreement between annotators, a third independent expert adjudicates. All annotators have ≥5 years of experience to ensure label consistency. Annotation protocol is documented and validated for inter-rater reliability (target Cohen's kappa ≥0.85) before labeling begins.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified split by clinic site and disease prevalence within each site. Temporal ordering is preserved within each stratum: 60% of images from each site/prevalence group for training (months 0–14), 20% for validation (months 15–18), 20% for test (months 19–24). This prevents temporal leakage while ensuring representativeness across sites and disease prevalence levels. Site-based stratification accounts for systematic differences in imaging equipment, patient demographics, and label practices across clinics.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformations (image normalization, histogram equalization, resizing to standard resolution) are fit exclusively on the training set statistics. Fitted parameters are then applied identically to validation and test sets. No image-level statistics (mean intensity, standard deviation, etc.) from validation or test sets inform preprocessing.
structured_choices.features: Raw high-resolution retinal images are passed directly to the CNN; no hand-crafted features are engineered. The CNN learns hierarchical representations end-to-end. For the baseline logistic regression model, we extract established clinical features: (1) presence of microaneurysms (detected via morphological operations), (2) hard exudate area (thresholded intensity), (3) hemorrhage count (connected component analysis), (4) vessel tortuosity (medial axis analysis), (5) optic disc size and location (Hough transform). Baseline features are standardized (z-score normalization fit on training set only).
structured_choices.model: CNN architecture: ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet, fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. The final fully-connected layer outputs a single sigmoid unit for binary classification. Hyperparameters: learning rate tuned via grid search (1e-5, 1e-4, 1e-3) on the validation set; batch size fixed at 32; optimizer is Adam; training uses early stopping based on validation loss (patience=10 epochs). No data augmentation is applied to preserve the integrity of the real-world image distribution.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on the same five hand-crafted clinical features. The baseline is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameter C (regularization strength) is tuned via grid search over the same validation set using the same evaluation metric. Both models receive identical train/val/test splits and identical preprocessing. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of learned CNN representations versus hand-engineered features, not differences in tuning or data access.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (recall / true positive rate) at a fixed specificity of ≥0.95. This directly aligns with the stakeholder's objective to minimize false negatives and enable early intervention. A threshold is selected on the validation set to achieve ≥0.95 specificity; this threshold is then applied to the test set without adjustment. Sensitivity is reported alongside 95% confidence intervals (Wilson score) to quantify uncertainty.
secondary_metrics: Specificity (to confirm the constraint is met on test data)
Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC), which is more informative than AUROC when false negatives carry asymmetric cost
Negative predictive value (NPV) to contextualize the clinical meaning of a negative prediction
Per-site sensitivity and specificity to detect performance heterogeneity across clinics
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameters for both CNN and baseline logistic regression are tuned using the validation set (20% of data). The metric used for model selection is sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity (or AUPRC as a secondary criterion for tie-breaking). The test set is held out and not used for tuning; it is evaluated exactly once after all hyperparameter tuning is complete.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once at the end of the experiment. No iteration, no post-hoc threshold adjustment, no adaptive re-weighting based on test performance. Point estimates and confidence intervals are reported; statistical significance testing (e.g., McNemar's test for sensitivity difference) is pre-specified before test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Ophthalmologist annotator experience is controlled by restricting all labelers to ≥5 years of experience and validating inter-rater reliability before label assignment. Per-annotator performance is tracked (sensitivity/specificity of each annotator against consensus labels) and reported to detect systematic bias from individual annotators. Stratified splits by clinic ensure that no single clinic dominates any split, which would otherwise allow clinic-specific label bias to correlate with the train/test boundary. A post-hoc check stratifies test set performance by annotator identity and clinic site to detect residual confounding.

design_narrative:
# Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Image Processing for Diabetic Retinopathy Detection

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses the full 50,000-image dataset collected from multiple outpatient clinics over two years. Prior to splitting, all images are screened for technical validity: corrupted files are discarded, and excessively poor quality images (e.g., severe blur, extreme underexposure) are flagged for expert review and excluded if non-diagnostic. Disease prevalence statistics and clinic site information are recorded for each image.

Labels are obtained through consensus annotation by ophthalmologists with ≥5 years of experience. For each image, two independent experts provide binary annotations (DR present/absent). If annotations agree, that label is assigned. If they disagree, a third expert adjudicates. Prior to labeling the full dataset, inter-rater reliability is validated on a held-out set of 500 images; only if Cohen's kappa ≥0.85 is achieved does the full annotation process proceed. This ensures label quality and reduces annotator-driven confounding.

## 2. Split Strategy

The data is divided using stratified random sampling by clinic site and disease prevalence (e.g., <10% prevalence, 10–30%, >30%). Within each stratum, images are sorted chronologically and split as follows:
- **Training set (60%):** Images from months 0–14 of the two-year collection period.
- **Validation set (20%):** Images from months 15–18.
- **Test set (20%):** Images from months 19–24.

This temporal ordering within strata prevents future information from leaking into training while respecting the site and prevalence stratification needed to ensure representativeness. Temporal validation is critical because disease presentation, imaging equipment, and clinic procedures may evolve; a strictly random split would not capture this drift. Stratification by site ensures that no single clinic dominates any split, preventing clinic-specific biases (e.g., a clinic with systematically lower image quality or different annotation practice) from confounding the model comparison.

## 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

**For the CNN model:**
All images are resized to 512×512 pixels and normalized using per-channel mean and standard deviation computed exclusively on the training set. No augmentation is applied; the model learns on the real-world distribution as-is. Normalization parameters are stored and applied identically to validation and test images.

**For the baseline logistic regression model:**
Five established clinical features are extracted from each image using classical image processing techniques:
1. Microaneurysm count (morphological closing + component labeling).
2. Hard exudate area (intensity thresholding + connected components).
3. Hemorrhage count (Hough-based red lesion detection).
4. Vessel tortuosity (medial axis skeleton analysis).
5. Optic disc size and center location (Hough circle transform).

Feature extraction is deterministic and uses no randomness. All features are standardized (z-score) using statistics fit on the training set; the same transformation is applied to validation and test sets. This ensures the baseline receives appropriate preprocessing care equivalent to the CNN pipeline.

## 4. Model Training and Baseline

**CNN Model:** ResNet-50 pretrained on ImageNet is fine-tuned end-to-end. The final layer is modified to output a single sigmoid unit. Training uses Adam optimizer, batch size 32, and early stopping (patience 10 epochs) based on validation loss. Hyperparameters—learning rate (grid search: 1e-5, 1e-4, 1e-3) and L2 regularization weight—are tuned on the validation set.

**Baseline Model:** Logistic regression trained on the five hand-crafted features. Hyperparameter C (inverse regularization strength) is tuned via grid search on the validation set over the range [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1, 10]. Both models receive identical train/validation/test splits and identical preprocessing. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of learned representations, not differences in tuning effort or data access.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Sensitivity (true positive rate) at a fixed specificity of ≥0.95. A threshold is chosen on the validation set to achieve this specificity target; the same threshold is applied to the test set without modification. This metric directly addresses the stakeholder's goal: minimizing missed cases (false negatives) while maintaining acceptable specificity. Sensitivity is reported with 95% Wilson score confidence intervals.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Specificity (to confirm the ≥0.95 constraint is met).
- Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC), which is more informative than AUROC when false negatives carry higher operational cost.
- Negative predictive value, quantifying the clinical safety of a negative prediction.
- Per-site and per-annotator sensitivity/specificity to detect performance heterogeneity and residual confounding.

## 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and model selection. Both the CNN and baseline are evaluated on the validation set using the primary metric (sensitivity at ≥0.95 specificity). Hyperparameters yielding the best validation performance are selected. The test set remains locked and untouched until final evaluation.

## 7. Test Set Evaluation and Confound Control

After all tuning is complete, the test set is evaluated exactly once. Performance is reported with point estimates and confidence intervals. A pre-specified statistical test (McNetar's test) compares the sensitivity of the two models on test data.

To control for ophthalmologist annotator bias, per-annotator and per-clinic performance is tracked. Test set results are stratified by annotator identity and clinic site to detect whether either model systematically outperforms in subpopulations or whether annotator bias influences the conclusion. This design establishes that the CNN outperforms logistic regression on diabetic retinopathy detection in the test population, but does not establish causality regarding dataset properties or real-world deployment effectiveness—only that learned representations provide better predictive accuracy under these specific controlled conditions.
